cards.answer: Parietal cells use H+/K+ ATPase (proton pump) to secrete H+ into the gastric lumen. Cl- follows via channels. Stimulated by ACh (vagus), histamine (ECL cells, via H2 receptors), and gastrin. Inhibited by somatostatin, prostaglandins, and secretin.

tip.tip: The proton pump is the target of PPIs.

cards.text: What are the functions of the pancreas in digestion?
cards.answer: Exocrine: secretes bicarbonate (neutralise acid) and enzymes (trypsinogen, chymotrypsinogen, lipase, amylase, elastase). Enterokinase in duodenum activates trypsinogen to trypsin, which activates others. Endocrine: insulin and glucagon from islets.

cards.text: What is the enterohepatic circulation of bile salts?
cards.answer: Bile salts secreted into duodenum -> emulsify fats in small intestine -> reabsorbed in terminal ileum (95%) -> portal vein -> liver -> re-secreted in bile. Only 5% lost in faeces. Pool recirculates 6-8 times per day.

tip.tip: A recycling system for bile salts.

cards.answer: Gastrin (G cells): stimulates acid secretion. CCK (I cells): gallbladder contraction, pancreatic enzymes. Secretin (S cells): pancreatic bicarbonate, inhibits gastric acid. GIP: stimulates insulin release. Motilin: MMC. Somatostatin: inhibits most GI hormones.

tip.tip: Each hormone has a specific trigger and target.

cards.text: How are carbohydrates digested and absorbed?
cards.answer: Salivary amylase begins starch digestion. Pancreatic amylase continues in duodenum -> maltose, maltotriose, dextrins. Brush border enzymes (maltase, sucrase, lactase) produce monosaccharides. Glucose and galactose absorbed via SGLT-1 (Na+ co-transport); fructose via GLUT-5.

Faeces enter rectum -> rectal distension -> intrinsic myenteric reflex + parasympathetic reflex (via pelvic splanchnic nerves S2-S4) -> internal anal sphincter relaxes. External sphincter (voluntary, pudendal nerve) relaxes consciously. Valsalva manoeuvre aids expulsion.

tip.tip: Internal sphincter is involuntary, external is voluntary.

cards.answer: Imbalance between aggressive factors (acid, pepsin, H. pylori, NSAIDs) and protective factors (mucus-bicarbonate barrier, prostaglandins, mucosal blood flow). H. pylori (most common cause in India) produces urease, damages mucosa. NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis.

Bile salts emulsify fat -> pancreatic lipase (with colipase) hydrolyses triglycerides to monoglycerides + fatty acids -> form micelles -> diffuse into enterocytes -> re-esterified to triglycerides -> packaged into chylomicrons -> enter lacteals (lymphatics).

tip.tip: Fats take the lymphatic route, not portal vein.
